literal: that which is heard; from √śru 'to hear'
invariant_definition: The class of authoritative texts comprising the four Vedas, the Brāhmaṇas, the Āraṇyakas, and the principal Upaniṣads — the texts considered *apauruṣeya* (not authored by any person, human or divine, in classical Mīmāṃsā and most Vedānta) and constitutive of *śabda-pramāṇa*. All Vedāntic schools accept *śruti* as the highest *pramāṇa* on matters beyond perception and inference, and especially on *brahman*. They differ on whether *śruti* is *apauruṣeya* in the strong Mīmāṃsā sense (Advaita follows this; Dvaita modifies it) or is the speech of the eternal Lord Viṣṇu (the Vaiṣṇava Vedāntins typically); on the canonical extent (whether the *Pañcarātra* counts; whether the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* counts as effectively *śruti*); and on the principles of interpretation (Mīmāṃsā's six *liṅgas* are universal; the schools differ in how they balance *upakrama-upasaṃhāra* with other indicators).

per_school.definition: *Śruti* is *apauruṣeya* in the strict Mīmāṃsā sense; its authority on *brahman* (the *jñāna-kāṇḍa*) is independent of any author. The *mahāvākyas* (especially *tat tvam asi*, *aham brahmāsmi*, *prajñānam brahma*, *ayam ātmā brahma*) are the principal liberating loci. *Śruti* is the unique *pramāṇa* for *brahman*; perception and inference cannot establish *brahman*.

*Śruti* is authoritative; the *Pañcarātra Saṃhitās* are accepted as functionally equivalent in authority for the worship of Viṣṇu. *Śruti* speaks of a *saviśeṣa brahman* throughout; the *nirguṇa* texts are read as denying *heya-guṇas*, not all qualities. The principle of interpretation foregrounds *sāmānādhikaraṇya* (co-reference) under *aprthak-siddhi*.

*Śruti* is the speech of Viṣṇu; its authority is absolute. Madhva's distinctive expansion of the canon includes *Pañcarātra*, *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa*, and *Mahābhārata* as primary; he also cites texts not preserved in the standard Vedic canon (the *Bhaviṣyat-Parva*, etc.) — a long-debated source-question. Interpretive principle: *brahman* is Viṣṇu, and every *śruti* statement is to be read consistently with this *abhipraya*.

*Śruti* is supplemented by the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa*, which is treated as the *natural commentary* on the *Brahma-Sūtra* (Jīva Gosvāmī's *Tattva-Sandarbha* §§17–28 establishes this). The *Bhāgavata*'s authority is therefore at least equal to the *Upaniṣads*' on the matters it treats — i.e., on Kṛṣṇa as *svayaṃ-bhagavān*.

translator_note: 'Scripture' is acceptable but imports Christian / People-of-the-Book connotations (a closed canon revealed at a moment in history) that do not fit. *Śruti* in the strict Mīmāṃsā-Advaita sense is *apauruṣeya* — uncreated, eternal — and 'heard' by the *ṛṣis* in deep meditation; this is structurally different from a divine dictation event. 'Heard-corpus' or simply *śruti* untranslated is best.
